1: Welcome. This is a warm and honest walkthrough about wellbeing after birth. The time with a newborn is big and overwhelming, and it is okay not to always feel well. We go through it at a calm pace, without shame.
2: We talk about baby blues and low mood, about feelings and signs to be aware of, about depression and anxiety after birth, and about the fact that the partner can be affected too. And then we go through what helps and where the help is. The most important thing first: it is okay not to feel well. It says nothing about how much you love your baby, and help is available.
3: Most new parents feel tearful and sensitive for a few days after birth. This is called baby blues and is tied to hormones, tiredness, and a big adjustment. It is usually at its most around day three to five and usually fades on its own within a couple of weeks. But if the low mood lasts longer than that, or feels deeper and heavier, then it is about more than baby blues, and it is worth seeking support.
4: The time after a birth often holds many feelings at once, sometimes completely opposite ones. It is common to burst into tears without a clear reason, to worry a lot about your baby, and to feel both overwhelming love and fear at the same time. Many feel inadequate or wait for a feeling of happiness that is slow to come. No feelings are wrong. What matters is how long they stay and how much they weigh on you.
5: The difference from baby blues is about how long it lasts and how much it takes over. Be aware of a low mood that persists for more than a couple of weeks, of joy and closeness failing to appear, of sleep faltering despite exhaustion, and of a constant worry and anxiety with restlessness and catastrophic thoughts. If you recognise several of these in yourselves or in each other, raise it with the child health centre (BVC) or the health centre. Don't wait until it gets worse.
6: Postpartum depression, or postnatal depression, affects many new parents. It can come creeping, weeks or months after the birth, and it is not something you have caused or can think your way out of. It is common, it is not your fault, and above all it is treatable. With talking support and sometimes medication the vast majority recover fully. Seeking help for your wellbeing is taking care of your baby, not letting it down.
7: Many new parents get sudden, frightening thoughts about something happening to their baby. They can feel scary and shameful, but they are common, and they do not mean that you are dangerous or will do something. A thought frightening you is something completely different from wanting to do what it is about. Putting the thoughts into words with the BVC or a doctor takes away some of their power. They have heard it before, and you don't have to carry them alone.
8: It is not only the one who gave birth who can struggle. The partner can also become low, worried, or exhausted, sometimes without noticing it themselves in the midst of everything new. Keep an eye on each other, ask how you are for real, and listen without immediately fixing. Share the nights and everyday life, because sleep is a protection for the wellbeing of you both. And to you as a partner: you don't have to have all the answers. Staying present and fetching help when it is needed is the most important thing you can do.
9: You don't have to do everything yourself, and not all at once. Sleeping in blocks, where you take turns so that each of you gets unbroken sleep, makes a big difference, because lack of sleep amplifies everything else. Say yes to help with food, laundry, and siblings. Put words to how you feel with someone you trust, because shame shrinks when it is shared. And don't hesitate to seek professional support. It is never too early to get in touch.
10: You never need to wait until it gets worse. If you feel low, worried, or not quite yourself, tell the BVC or the health centre, even if you are unsure. If the low mood, the anxiety, or the sleeplessness persists and begins to wear down your energy, seek help the same day. And if you have thoughts of harming yourself or your baby, or feel that you cannot go on living, call care or 112 straight away. Don't wait. You and your baby are worth emergency help, and it is there.
11: There are many doors in to support, and you only have to open one. The BVC knows you and can guide you onwards. The health centre is there for assessment, talking support, and treatment. 1177 gives advice around the clock by phone and online. If there is danger to life you call 112, and there are also support lines around the clock for difficult thoughts. Marie puts your local numbers here.
12: Asking for help is strength, not weakness. Ask yourselves and each other how you are, for real. Be kind to yourselves and get in touch early. You are not alone.
